macro_segments.title: The Debate on Fire Liability: Is Fire Considered Like Direct Action (Chitzav) or Like Property Damage (Mamono)?

micro_segments.title: Introduction: The Core Debate Between Rabbi Yochanan and Reish Lakish
Application to Fire Spread: If an Animal Lights a Fire, Who Pays Half Damages?
Reish Lakish's Position: Fire Spread as Your Own Property Damage, Not Direct Action

macro_segments.title: Case of Lighting a Haystack with a Bound Goat and Slave Nearby: When Are You Liable?

micro_segments.title: The Scenario: Haystack Burns with Bound Goat and Unbound Slave Present
The Slave Cannot Be Paid For: No Civil Liability for Killing a Human
Why the Goat Must Be Bound and the Slave Must Be Free
The Kim Lei Principle: When Murder Cancels Monetary Claims
Reish Lakish's Direct Torching Caveat: Only Kim Lei Applies When You Directly Set Fire to the Slave
Different Owners: The Goat and Slave Belong to Different People—Does Kim Lei Still Apply?

macro_segments.title: Textual Support for Rabbi Yochanan: The Pasuk and Braisa Identify Fire as Your Action

micro_segments.title: The Pasuk (Exodus 22:5-6): 'If Fire Spreads, the One Who Lights It Pays'
The Braisa: Torah Opens with Property Damage, Closes with You as the Doer

macro_segments.title: Abye's Challenge: If Fire Is Your Direct Action, Why Are You Exempt for Buried Items?

micro_segments.title: The Problem: Buried Wallet in Haystack—Why Exempt If Fire Is Like Your Arrow?
Resolution: The Fire's Trajectory Ends at the Wall—Beyond That Is Not Your Action
The Wall Falls from Earthquake: Your Original Negligence Stops, But You Remain Liable as Responsible for Negligence

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Yochanan's Synthesis: Both Chitzav and Mamono Apply to Fire Liability

micro_segments.title: Fire Has a Dual Nature: It Acts Like Your Arrow (Chitzav) Within Expected Bounds, but Falls Back to Property Damage (Mamono) When Exceeding Original Negligence
Major Practical Difference: Personal Injury from Fire Requires Payment of Four Damages (Negah, Tzaar, Shevet, Ripui, U'Voshet)

macro_segments.title: The Hagra's Reinterpretation: Fire Liability Rests on Negligence with Property, Not Direct Action

micro_segments.title: The Hagra Reads the Mishna: 'Opens with Property Damage, Closes with You as Doer' Means Your Negligence Makes You Liable as If You Did It Directly
The Foundation of Liability: Not That You Actually Did It, but That You Should Have Guarded Against It

macro_segments.title: The Dog, the Coal, and the Grain Sheaf: When the Owner of the Dog Pays Half Damages

micro_segments.title: Scenario: Dog Takes Owner's Coal and Causes a Fire in Someone Else's Haystack—Full and Half Damages
Was the Coal Owner Negligent? The Defense of Proper Guarding Against a Dog's Theft
Can a Normal Fence Stop a Dog? A Dog Digging Under Shows Negligence on Both Sides

macro_segments.title: The Dog, the Coal, and the Grain Sheaf: When the Owner of the Dog Pays Half Damages (continued)

micro_segments.title: Different Standards of Care: Higher Duty Not to Injure Others vs. Moderate Duty to Guard Your Own Goods

macro_segments.title: The Mouth as Domain: Does an Animal Eating in Its Own Mouth Constitute Eating in the Victim's Domain?

micro_segments.title: The Question: If a Dog Eats in a Third Party's Field, Is the Owner Liable (Reshut Hanizak) or Exempt (Reshut Hamazik)?
The Gemara's Conclusion: The Mouth Is Considered the Victim's Domain When Food Is Eaten There
The Apparent Contradiction: Earlier Cases Suggested Taking from One Domain and Eating Elsewhere Should Be Liable; Now It Seems to Require Eating in the Victim's Domain
Tosafot's Brilliant Insight: The Gemara Implicitly Asserts That Location of Eating (Not Just Taking) Determines Liability

topical_tags.term: Esh Mishum Chitzav (Fire as Direct Action)
Esh Mishum Mamono (Fire as Property Damage)
Rabbi Yochanan vs. Reish Lakish Debate
Kim Lei (Capital Crime Cancels Monetary Liability)
Cheresh, Shoteh, V'Katan (Deaf, Insane, Minor—Non-Responsible Persons)
Patur Midinei Adam V'Chayav B'Dinei Shamayim (Exempt in Court, Liable in Heaven)
Reshut Hanizak vs. Reshut Hamazik (Victim's Domain vs. Damager's Domain)
Negah, Tzaar, Shevet, Ripui, U'Voshet (Personal Injury Damages—Pain, Healing, Wages, Lost Earning Potential, Shame)
Adam Muad L'Olam (Person Is Always Presumed Liable for Harm They Cause)
Gzeirat HaKatuv (A Decree of Torah—Unexplained Scriptural Requirement)
Peshia HaNizak (Contributory Negligence of the Victim)
